coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with bright fluorescence for intracellular detection of iron ion and thiol
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiao-Yang
Li, Yu
Wang, Yu-Ying
Liu, Xing-Yu
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
Jiang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in recent years because of their excellent water solubility and abundant surface functional groups. However, compared with quantum dots or biological probes, the quantum yield of CDs is lower, and the fluorescence mainly concentrated in the blue-green range, which significantly limits the biological applications of CDs. Heteroatoms doping is the most common method to improve the luminescence of CDs. In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped luminescent CDs were successfully synthesized by microwave assisted method using glutathione (GSH) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as raw materials. It can emit bright green fluorescence in ethanol solution, and the maximum emission wavelength is 535 nm when excited at 374 nm, and the absolute quantum yield is as high as 63%. Iron ion (Fe3+) can interact with the functional groups on the surface of the CDs to form CDs/Fe3+, which is a non-fluorescence complex, and Fe3+ can be reduced to ferrous ion (Fe2+). In other words, the reaction mechanism of CDs and Fe3+ is a combination of dynamic quenching and static quenching. The fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ can be restored by thiol, because there is a stronger binding force between sulfhydryl (-SH) on the surface of thiol and Fe3+, which enables CDs to be released. In addition, the CDs has good biocompatibility and stability, indicating that it has excellent potential in bioimaging. This discovery will expand the application of CDs in the fields of biosensing and imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S co-doped carbon dots
Bight fluorescence
Fe3+ detection
Thiol detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Standard addition detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
Standard addition detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
Thermodynamics of the reaction between CDs and Fe3+
Detection of sulfhydryl compounds by CDs
Application of CDs in cell detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (a) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs in ethanol solution (inset: pictures of CDs under white light and UV light); (b) TEM of CDs (inset: HRTEM); (c) particle size distribution of CDs; (d) XRD spectrum of CDs.

FTIR and XPS spectra of CDs. (a) FTIR spectra of CDs; (b) XPS spectra of CDs; (c) High-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs; (d) High-resolution energy spectrum of N1s; (e) High-resolution O1s spectrum of CDs; (f) High-resolution energy spectrum of S2p.

Selectivity of CDs to Fe3+. (a) Influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs; (b) Influence of different amino acids on the fluorescence of CDs; (c) The quenching effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of CDs in the presence of other metal ions; (d) Photograph of the co-incubation of CDs with different metal ions under UV light.

Detection of Fe3+ by CDs. (a) Effect of Fe3+ in different concentrations on the fluorescence spectra of CDs; (b) The relationship between F 0/F and Fe3+ concentration (F 0 refers to the fluorescence intensity of 5 μg/mL of CDs at 452 nm; F represents the fluorescence intensity at 452 nm after interacting with different concentrations of Fe3+).

The reaction mechanism of CDs and Fe3+. (a) The fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs/Fe3+; (b) High-resolution Fe2p spectrum of CDs/Fe3+; (c) The changes in the UV–Vis absorption spectra after the interaction of the CDs with Fe3+; (d) The hydrodynamic diameter of CDs and CDs/Fe3+; (e) The change of FTIR spectrum of CDs after the interaction with Fe3+; (f) Zeta potential of CDs and CDs/Fe3+.

Detection of sulfhydryl amino acid by CDs/Fe3+. (a) Influence of 200 μM different amino acids on the fluorescence spectra of CDs; (b) Effect of GSH in different concentrations on the fluorescence spectra of CDs; (c) The relationship between F and GSH concentration (F represents the fluorescence intensity at 452 nm).

Application of CDs in cell imaging. (a) Effect of CDs on the viability of cancer cells (Hela and HL-60); (b) Effect of CDs on the viability of normal cells (COS-7 and MDCK); (c) Confocal image of Hela cells incubated with CDs for 6 h (first row) and then incubated with Fe3+ for 30 min (second row). Scale bar: 38 μm.
The fluorescence of CDs being quenched by Fe3+ and restored by thiol.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with bright fluorescence for intracellular detection of iron ion and thiol

R. China (Y. Liu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences & Key Laboratory of Combinatorial Biosynthesis and Drug Discovery (Ministry of Education)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in recent years because of their excellent water solubility and abundant surface functional groups. However, compared with quantum dots or biological probes, the quantum yield of CDs is lower, and the fluorescence mainly concentrated in the blue-green range, which significantly limits the biological applications of CDs. Heteroatoms doping is the most common method to improve the luminescence of CDs. In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped luminescent CDs were successfully synthesized by microwave assisted method using glutathione (GSH) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as raw materials. It can emit bright green fluorescence in ethanol solution, and the maximum emission wavelength is 535 nm when excited at 374 nm, and the absolute quantum yield is as high as 63%. Iron ion (Fe3+) can interact with the functional groups on the surface of the CDs to form CDs/Fe3+, which is a non-fluorescence complex, and Fe3+ can be reduced to ferrous ion (Fe2+). In other words, the reaction mechanism of CDs and Fe3+ is a combination of dynamic quenching and static quenching. The fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ can be restored by thiol, because there is a stronger binding force between sulfhydryl (-SH) on the surface of thiol and Fe3+, which enables CDs to be released. In addition, the CDs has good biocompatibility and stability, indicating that it has excellent potential in bioimaging. This discovery will expand the application of CDs in the fields of biosensing and imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most abundant transition metal element in the earthcrust,[1] iron is exist widely in the human body. It is an essential nutrient for organisms. It can participate in many biological metabolic processes, such as photosynthesis,[2] respiration,[3] nitrogen fixation [4] DNA biosynthesis,[5] oxygen transport,[6] electron transfer,[7] etc. Excessive or lack of iron content in the human body can lead to the occurrence of some diseases, such as Parkinson’s syndrome,[8] Alzheimer’s disease,[9] anemia[10] and cancer.[11] Iron is also an essential element for the formation of heme. Iron ion (Fe3+) combines with transferrin to form serum iron, then transported to the bone marrow or other iron-demanding tissues for use. As a kind of critical biochemical indicator, the serum iron content can objectively evaluate and diagnose many diseases.[12] In addition, in the “Standards for drinking water quality” promulgated by our country in 2007, it is clearly stipulated that the iron content in the water shall not exceed 0.3 mg/mL. Since ferrous ion (Fe2+) is unstable and can be easily oxidized to Fe3+, iron mainly exists in the form of Fe3+ in nature. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a sensitive, convenient, and low-cost Fe3+ detection method for actual water samples and organisms.

So far, there have been many reports in the literature about Fe3+ detection methods, including colorimetry,[13] atomic absorption spectrometry,[14] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry.[15] However, these methods have certain limitations, such as long test time, the need for complex sample preparation, and expensive experimental equipment. These shortcomings limit the broad application of the above methods. In recent years, the rapid development of fluorescence spectroscopy has received widespread attention at home and abroad because of its advantages of high sensitivity, convenience and good reproducibility.[16–18] At present, there have been reports in the literature that chemical sensors based on organic small molecule probes,[19] semiconductor quantum dots,[20] metal nanoclusters,[21] and metal–organic frameworks[22] have been used for the detection of Fe3+. However, these materials also have some shortcomings that cannot be ignored. For example, the preparation routes are commonly complicated, and heavy metal raw materials used in the synthesis process may cause environmental pollution, poor water solubility and poor photostability would also limit their biological applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a quasi-zero-dimensional carbon-based material with a size of less than 20 nm. Since it was first discovered in 2004,[23] it has been rapidly developed because of its good biocompatibility, good stability, wide range of raw materials, easy preparation, and low cost.[24–25] CDs are widely used in many aspects, such as catalysis,[26–27] bioimaging,[28] biomedicine,[29] information encryption[30] and antibacterial.[31] Due to its good optical properties and abundant surface functional groups, CDs are also widely used in biosensing.[32] However, compared with quantum dots and probes, CDs have some shortcomings, such as low quantum yield and the luminescence mainly concentrated in the range of blue-green light. To improve the luminescence properties of CDs, scientists have made a lot of efforts, such as doping with heteroatoms and increasing the conjugated structure.[33–35] Among them, the doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms is widespread. Literatures show that the doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms can effectively increase the quantum yield, reduce the energy gap, and make the luminescence redshift.[36–37]

In this work, we used glutathione (GSH) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as the carbon source and water as the solvent to successfully synthesize a nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs by microwave assisted method, and achieved the specific detection of Fe3+ and thiol. Fe3+ not only forms a complex with sulfhydryl (-SH) and amino (–NH2) group on the surface of the CDs, leading to the quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs, but also can be reduced by the CDs to form Fe2+. In other words, the reaction mechanism of CDs and Fe3+ is a combination of dynamic quenching and static quenching. In addition, the addition of thiol can restore the fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+, because the -SH rich in thiol has a stronger binding force with Fe3+, so that the CDs are released and the fluorescence of the CDs is restored (Scheme 1 ).

All purchased reagents and chemicals are used directly without further purification. The experimental water was deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) purified by Millipore system. GSH (98%), l-Cysteine (Cys, 98%), Homocysteine (Hcy, 95%), N-Acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC, 99%) was purchased from Sigma. The PPD (Chemically pure) and ferric chloride trihydrate (97%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China).

Cary fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to record the fluorescence changes of CDs. The absorption spectrum was measured by the Cary series ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The morphology and lattice information of the CDs are obtained by the JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Corporation, Japan). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy 5700 infrared spectrum analyzer (FTIR, Thermo Company, USA) was used to record Fourier transform infrared spectrum. ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the elemental composition of CDs. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs was obtained by the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS). The Zeta potential and hydrated particle size changes of CDs were recorded by Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Corporation, UK). The fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of CDs were tested by FLS 1000 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

GSH (0.334 g) and PPD (0.118 g) were ultrasonic dispersed in 10 mL HCl-NaOH aqueous solution (pH = 6) then transferred to a microwave reaction system and heated to 180℃ for 90 min. The obtained brown transparent solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. Then, the solution was adjusted to pH = 2 by dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl 1 M) and dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 8 h. Finally, the solution was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane, and freeze-dried to obtain a reddish-brown powder for further use.

In order to verify the selectivity of CDs to Fe3+, we selected 15 common interfering cations (Mn2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Cu+) to investigate the effects of other metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs. At room temperature 1 mM Fe3+ or other cations were incubated with CDs (0.2 mg/mL), respectively. The fluorescence spectrum of the solution (λ ex = 350 nm, λ em: 370–680 nm) was measured, and the fluorescence intensity at 452 nm was recorded. All experiments were repeated three times or more. In addition, the fluorescence emission photos of different metal ions mixed with CDs under ultraviolet light were recorded with a digital camera (Canon).

We first explored the changes in the fluorescence of CDs with the excitation wavelength. Afterwards, the excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm, and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs was recorded as a function of concentration, and the optimal concentration for detecting Fe3+ was determined. At room temperature, the 5 μg/mL CDs interacted with different concentrations of Fe3+. The excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at 452 nm was recorded by the fluorescence spectrometer. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The calculation formula of the limit of detection is LOD = 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation of 20 blank samples, and s is the slope of the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The final concentration of fixed CDs was 5 μg/mL, and Fe3+ was 10, 20, 35, 65, 100, 150 μM, respectively. The Fe3+ concentration was calculated according to the standard curve and compared with the actual concentration to calculate the fortified recoveries. It was found that the fortified recoveries were between 98% and 104%, which proved that CDs can be used for high-sensitivity detection of Fe3+ in real water samples. All experiments were repeated three times or more.

Fe3+ was added to the CDs solution (final concentration of CDs: 5 μg/mL, Fe3+: 200 μM) to quench the fluorescence of the CDs, and name the resulting solution CDs/Fe3+. Sulfhydryl amino acids (GSH, Cys, Hcy, NAC) were added to CDs/Fe3+ at a final concentration of 200 μM. The change in fluorescence intensity of CDs with the concentration of sulfhydryl amino acids is recorded. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

Cancer cells (Hela, HL-60) and normal cells (COS-7, MDCK) are cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin at 37℃ in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs. Cells were spread evenly in 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 103 per well, then put it in an incubator and culture for 12 h. After the cells adhere to the wall, DMEM medium containing a series of CDs was added in 96-well plate and continue to culture for 24 h. Finally, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT solution was added into each well for another 4 h. Carefully aspirate the solution in the 96-well plate, 150 μL of DMSO was added, and incubated at 37℃ for 30 min until the blue-violet formazan crystals are completely dissolved. The absorption at 490 nm was recorded by Elx800 Micropalte reader (BioTek, USA). All experiments were repeated three times or more.

Hela cells were inoculated in a confocal dish. After the cells adhered, 200 μg/mL CDs were added, and incubated in a 37℃ for 6 h. After the incubation, the cells were carefully washed 3 times with PBS, and the intracellular fluorescence signal (λ ex = 405 nm) was collected with a laser confocal microscope (PerkinElmer UltraVIEW VoX). In the Fe3+ detection experimental group, the cells were washed with PBS and 1 mM Fe3+ was added, and after incubating for 30 min, the fluorescence signal of the cells was collected.

In ethanol solution, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength is λ ex/λ em = 374/535 nm of CDs when the concentration is 200 μg/mL, as shown in Fig. 1a. The solution is a pale yellow transparent liquid under natural light and shows bright green fluorescence when illuminated by 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, as shown in the illustration. The absolute quantum yield in ethanol solution is as high as 63%. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) of the CDs shows that the CDs are spherical nanoparticles with a particle size distribution between 2.8 and 5.4 nm and an average particle size of 3.9 ± 0.4 nm, as shown in Fig. 1c. The HRTEM image in the inset shows that the lattice spacing of the CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, which indicates that the CDs have a graphite-like structure core.[37] As shown in Fig. 1d, the X-ray powder diffraction spectrum of CDs shows a broad absorption peak at 2θ = 22.85° (0.43 nm), which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating that CDs have satisfactory crystallization properties.[32] The slightly larger layer spacing than the 0.34 nm of graphite is caused by the introduction of more oxygen-containing functional groups.

As shown in Figure S1b, in the aqueous solution, the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the CDs shows two absorption peaks, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively.[29,32] The fluorescence spectrum in the figure shows that when the concentration of CDs is 200 μg/mL, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength is λ ex/λ em = 350/481 nm. As shown in Figure S1c, when the excitation wavelength of the CDs is gradually changed from 310 nm to 450 nm (at an interval of 10 nm), the emission peak positions of the CDs are also gradually red-shifted. The CDs exhibit excitation-dependence emission property. According to reports in the literature,[38] there are two types of luminescence centers of CDs, surface functional groups and carbon nuclear. The luminescence of carbon nuclear is excitation-independent, while the luminescence dominated by surface functional groups is excitation-dependent. As shown in Figure S1d, when the excitation wavelength is 365 nm, the emission peak position gradually redshifts with the increase of CDs concentration, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs reaches the maximum when the concentration is 200 μg/mL.

In order to explore the stability of CDs, we tested the luminescence performance of CDs under different pH conditions. As shown in Figure S3a, the CDs exhibit stable fluorescence at pH 3–12. When the CDs are dissolved in NaCl solutions of different concentrations, the fluorescence of the CDs is not significantly quenched (Figure S3b), which indicates that the CDs have good stability under high ionic strength. This endows CDs with the potential to detect Fe3+ under extreme conditions. Similarly, when the CDs are irradiated continuously for 120 min (Figure S3c) or continuously excited 100 times (Figure S3d), the fluorescence of the CDs remains stable, indicating that the CDs have excellent photostability.

The surface chemical composition and element state of CDs are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Figure S4, the significant difference in FTIR spectra of GSH, PPD and CDs represents the successful synthesis of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the broad peak at the high wavenumber of 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching vibration of the CDs. The two broad peaks at 3000 cm−1 and the absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the CH bond. The asymmetric stretching vibration of CO shows a sharp absorption peak at 1700 cm−1. The spike at 1500 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of CC. The stretching vibration of CN appears as an absorption peak at 1300 cm−1. The stretching vibration of NH can be found at the absorption peaks at 900 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1. We can also see an absorption peak at 2600 cm−1, which is caused by the stretching vibration of SH. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy shows that the surface of the CDs contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, and the surface of the CDs retains the sulfhydryl group of glutathione, which makes the CDs have a certain reduction ability and can combine with Fe3+.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the XPS diagram indicates that the CDs have four distinct bond energy peaks, located at 284.8 eV, 399.6 eV, 531.5 eV, 163.6 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, representing the CDs are composed of C, N, O, and S. The element content is shown in Table S1. We perform peak fitting of the high-resolution energy spectrum of each element. As shown in Fig. 2c, C1s is divided into three peaks, which are located at 284.7 eV, 285.4 eV, 288.2 eV corresponding to CC, CC/CN and COOH, and the appearance of oxygen-containing functional group COOH gives CDs favorable water solubility. As shown in Fig. 2d, the high-resolution energy spectrum of N1s can be fitted to three peaks, located at 398.7 eV, 399.7 eV and 401.3 eV, which can be assigned to CNC, NC and NH. As shown in Fig. 2e, the high-resolution energy spectrum of O1s is fitted into two peaks, namely CO at 531.5 eV and COC/C-OH at 533.2 eV. For the high-resolution energy spectrum of S2p (Fig. 2f), we divide it into three peaks, located at 163.5 eV, 164.7 eV, 168.3 eV which correspond to S2p3/2, S2p1/2, SO, respectively.[37] The XPS analysis results are consistent with the FTIR analysis results, which can corroborate each other.

The surface of CDs contains many functional groups. It has been reported in the literature that CDs can specifically recognize metal ions such as Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and so on.[32,39–40] In order to verify the selectivity of CDs to iron ions, we tested the changes in fluorescence intensity after CDs interacted with several common metal ions and amino acids. As shown in Fig. 3 a, after the CDs are incubated with metal ions, only Fe3+ significantly quench the fluorescence of the CDs, while other metal ions have no significant effect on the fluorescence of the CDs. Similarly, when the CDs are incubated with amino acids, the fluorescence of the CDs does not change significantly, as shown in Fig. 3b. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3c, when other metal ions coexist with Fe3+, Fe3+ can still efficiently quench the fluorescence of CDs, and the quenching degree is not affected by other metal ions, indicating that CDs have excellent selectivity to Fe3+. This phenomenon was visualized by the fluorescence photos of the solution under 365 nm UV excitation after co-incubation of CDs and metal ions (Fig. 3d), and it is evident that only Fe3+ could almost completely quench the fluorescence of CDs. The above experimental results indicate that CDs have the potential to detect Fe3+ with high selectivity.

In order to obtain the best detection effect, we first optimized the relevant conditions for CDs detection of Fe3+. As shown in Figure S5a, when the concentration is in the range of 0–50 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increases linearly with the increase of concentration. In order to obtain a lower detection limit, we select CDs of 5 μg/mL for the following experiment. We also studied the effect of pH changes on the degree of quenching. As mentioned before, the fluorescence of CDs can remain stable in a wide pH range. As shown in Figure S5b, Fe3+ has a satisfactory quenching effect on the fluorescence of CDs when the pH is 4–10. Considering the application of CDs in cell fluorescence imaging, pH = 6 is selected as the following experimental condition. Finally, the response time of Fe3+ quenching the fluorescence of CDs is investigated. As shown in Figure S5c, when 200 μM Fe3+ is added, the fluorescence intensity of CDs will rapidly decrease, and the quenching will be complete within 2 min. After that, the fluorescence of CDs will almost remain unchanged as time goes by. Combined with the above experimental results, we finally selected 5 μg/mL CDs, pH = 6, and a response time of 2 min as the best conditions for detecting Fe3+.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, we evaluated the ability of CDs to analyze and detect Fe3+ quantitatively. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreases with the addition of Fe3+, showing a typical concentration-dependent behavior. When the concentration of Fe3+ increased to 200 μM, the fluorescence of the CDs utterly quenched. When the concentration of added Fe3+ is in the range of 0–150 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs has an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of added Fe3+ (Fig. 4b). As the concentration of Fe3+ continues to increase, the data begins to deviate from the initial linearity. By fitting the above linear relationship, the following equation (eq (1)) can be obtained to detect the concentration of Fe3+ quantitatively:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 0.00799
C
+ 1.02
R
2
= 0.995
where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs, F is the fluorescence intensity of CDs with Fe3+, [C] is the concentration of Fe3+. The calculated LOD is 1.2 μM. This is lower than the maximum content of Fe3+ in drinking water allowed in the “Standards for drinking water quality”, so this CDs is expected to be used for the detection of Fe3+ in drinking water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The above experimental results all show that the specific identification and detection of Fe3+ reaction system based on CDs has excellent performance such as high selectivity, high sensitivity, rapid response, simple operation, pollution-free, which makes the system expected to be extended to the detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples. The tap water sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particles of insoluble matter. We added different concentrations of Fe3+ to tap water as an internal standard. As shown in Table 1 , the measured recovery rates are all between 99% − 104%. It proves that the accuracy of the standard curve is high and the test result is credible.

Next, the reaction mechanism of the interaction between CDs and Fe3+ was explored. The most accurate method to distinguish between static quenching and dynamic quenching is to test the change of fluorescence lifetime. Therefore, fluorescence lifetime was measured to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs triggered by Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after interacting with 200 μM Fe3+, the lifetime of the CDs decreases from 7.6 ns to 6.6 ns, and as the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the lifetime of CDs gradually decreases. The results are shown in Table S2, indicating that the quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs by Fe3+ is dynamic.[41] We further tested the XPS spectrum of CDs/Fe3+. Figure S6 showed that the CDs/Fe3+ system contains five elements: C, N, O, S, and Fe. The element content is shown in Table S1. We performed peak fitting of the high-resolution energy spectrum of Fe, as shown in Fig. 5b. The Fe2p diagram indicates that it can be divided into Fe2+ (709.8 eV, 723.2 eV) and Fe3+ (714.2 eV, 728.2 eV), [42] the above results prove that Fe2+ formed after interacting with CDs, further confirming that the quenching process is dynamic quenching. Besides this, we further tested the changes in the UV–Vis absorption spectra after the interaction of the CDs with Fe3+. Unexpectedly, the CDs showed a new absorption peak at 364 nm after the addition of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 5c. This means that there are complex formations in the quenching process.[32] We further tested the changes in the hydrodynamic diameter in the quenching process. As shown in Fig. 5d, we found that after interaction with Fe3+, the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs/Fe3+ increased to 135 nm, which far exceeded the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs (7 nm). We can see that there are two peaks of CDs/Fe3+. The small particle size peak is caused by the presence of a small amount of incompletely reacted CDs. The increase of hydrodynamic diameter was caused by the formation of complexes between CDs and Fe3+. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 5e), we can clearly see that the intensity of -SH and –NH2 on the surface of the CDs is significantly weakened after interaction with Fe3+. We defined the transmittance at 3800 cm−1 as 100%, and calculated the relative transmittance of the two samples. As shown in table S3, the higher light transmittance of CDs/Fe3+ means that the content of –NH2 and -SH is lower than that of CDs. This can be interpreted as Fe3+ can undergo oxidation–reduction reaction with the CDs to form Fe2+, and at the same time interact with the functional groups on the surface of the CDs to form CDs/Fe3+, which is a complex with non-fluorescence. The test result of Zeta potential (Fig. 5f) shows that the Zeta potential of CDs in aqueous solution is −10.8 mV, indicating that CDs can form complex compounds by electrostatic interaction with Fe3+. In summary, Fe3+ quenching the fluorescence of CDs is a mixed process of static quenching and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamics of the reaction between CDs and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to analyze the interaction process between CDs and Fe3+ from a more fundamental thermodynamic point of view, we study the driving force of the interaction between CDs and Fe3+. We tested the difference in the degree of quenching of the CDs fluorescence by Fe3+ at different temperatures, as shown in Figure S7a. The dissociation constant (K d) was obtained through the Hill equation (eq (2)):[43]
Q /
Q
max
=
C
n
/
(
K
d
n
+
C
n
)
(2)
Q = (
F 0
- F ) / F
where Q max is the maximum value of Q, K d is the dissociation constant. Then, the binding constant K a is obtained by Equation (3) (eq (3)):
(3)
K a
=
1
K d

As shown in Figure S7b, the relationship between K a and temperature was analyzed through Van’t Hoff equation (eq (4)):[44]
ln
K a
= -
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R
(4)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S
where ΔH is the enthalpy change, ΔS is the entropy change, ΔG is the Gibbs free energy, R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T represents temperature. The thermodynamic parameters obtained in the reaction process are shown in Table S4. The enthalpy change and entropy change are both greater than zero (ΔH > 0; ΔS > 0). According to Ross theory, it is speculated that the interaction between CDs and Fe3+ is mainly hydrophobic.[45] In order to further verify the results of fluorescence titration experiment at different temperatures, we conducted further analysis on the interaction process of CDs and Fe3+ through Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC), as shown in Figure S8. Not surprisingly, ΔH and ΔS obtained by ITC (Table S5) are positive, which can support the aforementioned conclusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of sulfhydryl compounds by CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
We added amino acid to the solution of CDs/Fe3+ to measure the fluorescence change. As shown in Fig. 6 a, four sulfhydryl amino acids (GSH, Hcy, Cys, NAC) all lead to the fluorescence recovery, but other amino acids have no significant changes to the fluorescence of the CDs/Fe3+ system. This is because the -SH has of sulfhydryl amino acids have a stronger binding capacity with Fe3+, so that CDs can be released and the fluorescence recovered. It shows that CDs/Fe3+ have the ability to recognize sulfhydryl amino acids specifically. We added different concentrations of GSH to the CDs/Fe3+ system and incubated it overnight at 37℃ to test the changes in fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 6b, as the concentration of GSH added increases, the fluorescence gradually recovers. When the concentration of GSH is 200 μM, the fluorescence of the CDs basically returns to the initial value. Analyzing the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 452 nm and the concentration of GSH (Fig. 6c), a standard curve (eq (5)) was obtained:
(5)
F = 875.8 - 697.2
e
-
C
GSH
169.7
(
R
2
= 0.998 )
where F is fluorescence intensity, [C]GSH is the concentration of GSH. [C]GSH in the sample can be calculated by evaluating the standard curve for the value of fluorescence intensity. In addition to the quantitative detection of GSH, the CDs/Fe3+ system can also realize the quantitative detection of Cys, Hcy, and NAC, as shown in Figure S9. The above results indicate that the fluorescence of CDs in the CDs/Fe3+ system can be specifically restored by sulfhydryl amino acids, and the quantitative detection of four sulfhydryl amino acids can be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs in cell detection
ce:section.ce:para: Studies have shown that Fe3+ is closely related to human health, so it is imperative to detect the content of Fe3+ in cells. Therefore, we investigated the potential of CDs to detect Fe3+ at the cellular level. We first tested whether the CDs would affect cell viability, and the MTT method was used for detection. The CDs were co-incubated with two kinds of cancer cells (Hela, HL-60) and two normal cells (MDCK, COS-7) for 24 h. The test found that the activity of cancer cells (Fig. 7 a) and (Fig. 7b) normal cells are maintained at>90%. The CDs did not cause a significant decrease in cell viability, indicating that the CDs have good biocompatibility. Next, the potential of CDs to detect intracellular Fe3+ was explored in Hela cells. As shown in Fig. 7c, after incubating the CDs with the cells for 6 h, the intracellular fluorescence signal was collected by a laser confocal microscope. Bright fluorescence can be seen in the cell, indicating that the CDs have the potential as a cell imaging reagent. However, when the incubated cells were treated with Fe3+ for 30 min, there is almost no fluorescence signal in the cell. The fluorescence in the cells could be quenched by Fe3+, indicating that the CDs can achieve the detection of Fe3+ at the cellular level.

In this article, we synthesized a multifunctional CDs with bright fluorescence and performed a comprehensive characterization. The abundant functional groups on the surface of the CDs enable the CDs to form micelles in the solution, so that the CDs have sufficient thermodynamic stability. It is used as a nano-sensor for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and thiol. We have conducted a comprehensive study on the detection mechanism by means of fluorescence titration experiment and ITC. The quenching of the fluorescence of CDs by Fe3+ is carried out by a combination of static and dynamic quenching. The above detection methods and quenching mechanism are rare in previous articles.[46–47] This discovery will expand the application of CDs in the field of biological imaging and sensing, and it provides new ideas for the future research of detection mechanism with CDs as a nano-sensor.

Xiao-Yang Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yu Li: Validation, Data curation. Yu-Ying Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xing-Yu Liu: Software. Feng-Lei Jiang: Supervision, Project administration. Yi Liu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Peng Jiang: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22073070, 22074113, 21873075), Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province (2016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.12.069.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
